recommended_next_actions: Keep Support Cockpit as the selected Starlight publishing direction now that the older legacy page structures have been cleaned out of the real review site.
Treat the current ASMS UI page as a discovery milestone and rebuild `ASMS UI is down` from scratch around useful-work questions instead of incrementally extending the current service-first structure.
Use the new shared Host Health Pre-Check pack at the start of every major playbook so Linux resource checks stay consistent before subsystem-specific diagnosis.

Use the current ASMS UI discovery work as source material for the rebuild: host health, Apache edge, Keycloak auth checks, ms-metro app checks, and the current dependency-order hypothesis are all inputs to the next clean playbook structure.
Use the current ASMS UI working model as host health -> Apache edge -> parallel first-pass Keycloak and ms-metro checks until bounded lab experiments prove a stricter serial dependency order.

Keep expanding service-specific command coverage, Linux note blocks, and known working terminal examples for the highest-value AlgoSec services and dependencies while the current Support Cockpit layout is stable, using the ASMS UI service-pack pattern as the first reusable model.
Plan for more of the agent memory, discovery, validation, and playbook-refinement loop to live inside the build-pack so remote Codex can iterate with less scenario-by-scenario manual steering.
Use subsystem-level paths like FireFlow Backend as the unit of command coverage when that gives a more accurate diagnostic model than isolated service-by-service checks.
Deepen dependency-aware playbooks after the command coverage pass so the next real paths inherit stronger operational checks and clearer failure branches.
Tune operator language after those content-depth passes so the wording cleanup lands on a richer and more stable diagnostic surface.
Keep growing the collapsible Linux note pattern as an optional learning layer so engineers can pick up Linux concepts without slowing down the main troubleshooting flow.

Decide whether ADF should formally treat Linux troubleshooting as a project-wide foundation, not only as an agent-persona idea, so future playbooks and discovery logic stay aligned with the real operating context.
Keep the ADF agent-persona alignment idea as a planned follow-up so the remote Codex behavior can later be reviewed against ADF's Linux troubleshooting and diagnostic-authoring goals.
Use the recorded remote invocation notes on the next lab run so stale process cleanup, non-login shell launching, and result-file verification happen in a repeatable way.
For the ASMS UI path, keep ActiveMQ as a supporting dependency unless ms-metro logs or behavior point directly to JMS or broker trouble.
Keep the Rocky lab as the proving ground for publishing changes while preserving the option to move the rendered Starlight site to an Ubuntu host later.

required_gates.summary: Build-pack contract validation passed after the Support Cockpit direction and prototype-generator updates were added to the current ADF runtime.
The existing smoke benchmark still passes while the build-pack adds the Support Cockpit prototype direction and refreshed Starlight prototype generation path.

planning_items.title: Formalize a shared host health pre-check pack
planning_items.summary: A fixed shared host health pre-check pack is now defined for every major playbook before subsystem-specific checks.
planning_items.why_it_matters: Disk, memory, OOM, and other host-resource checks are foundational Linux triage steps in every scenario. Defining them as a standard reusable pre-check keeps playbooks consistent and helps engineers start in the same place every time.
notes: Treat this as a shared command pack that appears at the start of major playbooks.
Prefer human-readable command forms such as `-h` or `--human-readable` where supported.
Use the host pre-check pack before subsystem-specific commands for ASMS UI, FireFlow Backend, Messaging and Data, and similar playbooks.
The fixed v1 command set and wording now live in `docs/specs/adf-host-health-precheck-pack-v1.md`.

planning_items.title: Explore a scenario-based dashboard direction for ADF
planning_items.summary: Consider whether ADF should gradually evolve from a documentation-style playbook surface into a small scenario-based clickable dashboard for support engineers.
planning_items.why_it_matters: ADF is starting to feel like a support engineer's support engineer. A bounded dashboard model could help engineers choose a likely starting scenario quickly and then work through collapsible diagnostic paths with less friction during live cases.
notes: Treat this as a future design direction, not an immediate UI rewrite.
Keep the number of starting scenarios intentionally small, such as three to five high-value paths.
Preserve the command-first troubleshooting flow and avoid turning the page into a crowded control panel.
Use scenario descriptions that help an engineer decide whether the path matches the current case.

planning_items.title: Build more autonomous agent iteration into ADF
planning_items.summary: Use the memory and feedback-loop surfaces already present in the build-pack, then decide what additional iteration logic should remain pack-local versus be imported from the factory itself so the remote agent can iterate with less scenario-by-scenario manual steering.
planning_items.why_it_matters: System-thinking playbooks are too deep to manage manually one scenario at a time. Much of the needed memory and feedback-loop foundation already exists in the build-pack, so the next design question is how to use that well and what should be inherited from factory-level logic.
notes: Keep this bounded to pack-local memory, approved lab evidence, and explicit planning state.
Treat factory imports as a possible source of shared iteration logic rather than assuming the loop must be rebuilt entirely inside ADF.
Treat this as build-pack logic and orchestration, not only as a remote runtime convenience.
Do not turn this into uncontrolled autonomy or open-ended scenario sprawl.
Keep human review in the loop for major design, scope, and validation decisions.

planning_items.title: Plan controlled fault-injection playbook validation
planning_items.summary: Consider a future validation mode where an agent can intentionally disable a service in the lab, then a second agent follows the playbook to see whether the failure path is discovered correctly.
planning_items.why_it_matters: This could become a powerful way to test whether a diagnostic playbook really works end to end instead of only reading well on the page. It would help prove that the dependency path, commands, and stopping points lead an agent or engineer to the right conclusion.
notes: Keep this far-future and lab-only for now.
Treat this as controlled fault injection, not a normal support workflow.
Require clear safety boundaries so only approved non-production lab targets can be mutated.
Use one agent to create the failure scenario and another to follow the playbook independently.

planning_items.title: Refine command palette contrast
planning_items.summary: Adjust the command palette styling so command text stays readable against the current code-block background and syntax-highlighting colors.
planning_items.why_it_matters: Some command text is hard to see because the syntax highlighting and frame background are too close in value. That creates friction exactly where engineers need the clearest visual surface.
notes: Keep this as a presentation-layer change, not a command-content change.
Review both the bash command blocks and the known working example blocks.
Prefer contrast improvements that keep the command area visually distinct without overpowering the rest of the page.

planning_items.title: Deepen ASMS UI service command packs
planning_items.summary: Expand the `ASMS UI services` step beyond simple service-up and port-open checks so Keycloak and ms-metro become real diagnostic command packs.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The current ASMS UI playbook is useful through host health and Apache edge, but the service-layer checks are still too shallow. Support engineers need service-specific commands that help explain why the UI is still failing, not only whether a process exists.
notes: Treat the current Step 3 as the immediate command-coverage gap revealed by live review.
Build richer packs for `keycloak.service` and `ms-metro.service` with status, listener, recent logs, and one or more service-specific functional checks.
Keep the commands read-only, plain-language, and junior-support friendly.
Do this before moving on to FireFlow Backend so the first subsystem path becomes genuinely useful.

planning_items.title: Rebuild ASMS UI is down around system thinking
planning_items.summary: Reset `ASMS UI is down` and rebuild it from scratch around useful-work questions instead of continuing the current service-first shape.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The current page proved several valuable patterns, but it is still not aligned with the stronger system-thinking theme. Rebuilding it cleanly is the fastest path to the first true ADF reference playbook.
notes: Start from the shared Host Health Pre-Check pack and then move through Apache/HTTPD serving the UI, auth branch, app branch, and useful-work stop point questions.
Treat Keycloak as an auth/login branch and `ms-metro` as an app branch rather than as isolated service rows.
Keep logs as supporting clue surfaces after the functional checks instead of as the main diagnostic destination.
Use the current ASMS UI page only as a discovery milestone and command-source reference during the rebuild.

planning_items.title: Map the ASMS UI subsystem and validate core scenarios
planning_items.summary: Build a bounded dependency map for the ASMS UI subsystem from lab evidence, then validate a small set of common failure scenarios against that map.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The ASMS UI path likely depends on a small set of important services such as httpd, keycloak, ms-metro, and possibly activemq, but the relationship needs to be mapped from evidence instead of guessed. A bounded map plus a few high-value scenarios will make the playbook far more trustworthy than shallow service checks or unlimited speculative branching.
notes: Start by mapping the ASMS UI subsystem from configs, listeners, service units, process relationships, and logs on the lab appliance.
Treat likely direct dependencies and likely supporting dependencies separately until the evidence is clearer.
After the map is stable, validate only a small scenario set such as host pressure, httpd down, keycloak down, ms-metro down, and one additional dependency case if the evidence supports it.
Keep this bounded to the reduced Rocky footprint and the ASMS UI subsystem rather than expanding into full-system reverse engineering.

planning_items.title: Validate ASMS UI dependency order experimentally
planning_items.summary: Confirm whether the ASMS UI path is truly ordered as host -> httpd -> keycloak -> ms-metro, or whether some of those services should remain peer first-pass checks instead of a strict serial chain.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The current ASMS UI playbook now has effective service packs, but the exact dependency order is not fully proven yet. ADF should only claim a strict path after lab evidence and bounded experiments confirm it.
notes: Treat the current playbook order as a strong working hypothesis, not a final proven dependency chain.
Use live evidence, config mapping, functional checks, and small lab experiments to confirm which service is upstream or downstream in the real ASMS UI path.
Keep ActiveMQ out of the first-pass path unless the evidence shows it is a direct gate for the UI-down scenario.
If controlled service-disable scenarios are needed later, keep them lab-only and align them with the broader controlled fault-injection planning item.

planning_items.title: Consider a horizontal dependency path in the center pane
planning_items.summary: The dependency path now renders horizontally within the center pane so it uses width instead of pushing the stepwise details farther down the page.
planning_items.why_it_matters: This recovers vertical room for the detailed checks while preserving the path-map view at the top of the playbook.
notes: Keep the dependency path inside the main center content area.
Preserve click-to-jump behavior to the matching step or checkpoint.
The horizontal strip is now live on the lab review page and remains a candidate for further tuning after operator review.

planning_items.title: Formalize subsystem-level command coverage
planning_items.summary: Capture the idea that ADF command coverage should expand and contract around subsystem-level dependency paths such as FireFlow Backend, not just isolated services.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The appliance and the application are both made of bounded modules and dependency paths. Support engineers diagnose those paths as subsystems, so command coverage and playbook structure should reflect that reality.
notes: Keep the model bounded to the reduced Rocky footprint and the important application subsystems that run on it.
Allow command packs to group services, ports, logs, scripts, Java processes, and Linux checks that belong to one subsystem-level path.
Use this idea to guide both command coverage and deeper playbook design without broadening into full-system reverse engineering.

planning_items.title: Rename Appliance UI is down to ASMS UI is down
planning_items.summary: The operator-facing playbook label now uses `ASMS UI is down` instead of `Appliance UI is down`.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The support-facing subject is now aligned with AlgoSec Security Management Suite naming on the live review surface, the generated baseline, and the Starlight playbook path.
notes: The rename is now applied in the generated baseline, symptom lookup, navigation labels, and the live Starlight playbook path.
Keep the meaning tied to the customer-visible ASMS user interface path.

planning_items.title: Enhance command-pack language
planning_items.summary: Refine the operator-facing language used inside the command packs now being built so the checks, verification text, and Linux notes stay plain, consistent, and easy to scan.
planning_items.why_it_matters: As the shared host health pre-check pack and subsystem-level command packs grow, the wording inside those packs needs to stay as deliberate as the command selection itself.
notes: Keep this focused on command-pack phrasing rather than the broader playbook narrative.
Use the ADF language standard as the base, but allow command-pack-specific refinements where they improve readability.
Apply this after or alongside the command coverage build-out so the language lands on the real command set.

planning_items.title: Enhance Linux notes as an optional learning layer
planning_items.summary: Expand the collapsible Linux note pattern with more short educational context so junior engineers can learn unfamiliar Linux terms without blocking the main troubleshooting flow.
planning_items.why_it_matters: The current Linux notes are useful because they are optional. Engineers can skip them when they already know the concept, or open them when they need a quick explanation. This can build shared Linux troubleshooting language over time without turning the playbook into training material.
notes: Keep Linux notes collapsible by default.
Prefer short plain-language explanations tied directly to the current check.
Use Linux notes to clarify terms like memory pressure, inode pressure, OOM, file locks, and similar concepts when they matter to triage.

planning_items.title: Codify system thinking as an ADF theme
planning_items.summary: Make ADF intentionally push engineers toward system thinking, where the goal is to determine whether a component, subsystem, or the whole system can do useful work, not only whether a process is up.
planning_items.why_it_matters: This is the deeper value behind the recent service-pack work. ADF is strongest when it helps engineers judge productive system behavior rather than memorize isolated service checks.
notes: Use this theme to shape command coverage, dependency paths, Linux notes, and future playbook wording.
Prefer checks that prove useful work or functional health over checks that only prove process presence.
Keep this bounded to support diagnostics and avoid turning ADF into a generalized systems-theory product.

planning_items.title: Formalize the Linux foundation as an ADF project theme
planning_items.summary: Decide how explicitly ADF should treat Linux troubleshooting as a core project theme, not just an agent-persona trait, because support engineers diagnose both the Linux operating system and the application stack built on top of Linux services and open source components.
planning_items.why_it_matters: ADF is already trending toward Linux-first diagnostics, Linux-standard language, and Linux note clarifications. Formalizing that foundation would help align playbooks, terminology, discovery logic, and future agent guidance around the real operating context engineers face during triage.
notes: Consider whether this belongs in project context, operator language standards, and future agent guidance rather than only inside a persona note.
Preserve the distinction between troubleshooting the Linux host itself and troubleshooting the application stack that depends on Linux services, ports, filesystems, logs, and open source components.
Use this theme to guide future playbook structure, dependency mapping, and educational Linux notes without turning ADF into a generic Linux training product.
